title: Listening
text:
Because this project focuses on video game music, this page provides a brief listening companion to the timeline and data analysis. These examples illustrate how soundtrack style changed across technological eras.


Early game music relied on short looping melodies and a limited number of sound channels.




This example highlights the short-loop structure and simple electronic timbres associated with early hardware limitations.





Improved sound chips and sampling allowed for richer textures and more stylistic variety.




This track reflects the faster rhythms and expanded musical palette of the 16-bit era.





As storage expanded, soundtracks became clearer, longer, and more cinematic.




This example shows how music increasingly supported atmosphere, world-building, and emotional tone.





Modern games include both large orchestral scores and more ambient, minimalist approaches.




Halo demonstrates the rise of cinematic orchestral scoring in modern games.








Minecraft offers a different modern approach: minimalist ambient composition used to create reflection and atmosphere.

title: Data
text:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

df <- read.csv("video_game_ost_cleaned_with_seconds.csv")

head(df)

title: Data
text:
Data Overview
This project uses a manually constructed dataset of video game soundtrack tracks drawn from representative games across multiple technological eras. Rather than attempting to include every video game soundtrack, I selected a set of influential and recognizable titles that help illustrate how soundtrack structure changed over time.
The dataset was designed to support a comparative analysis of video game music across historical periods, from early hardware-constrained compositions to modern cinematic and ambient scores.

title: Data
text:
How the Data Was Collected
The soundtrack data was collected manually from publicly available soundtrack listings and video game music databases. For each selected game, I recorded the individual track titles and their track lengths. Each track was entered as a separate observation in the dataset.
Games were chosen to represent major shifts in game music history, including:

early arcade and 8-bit sound design
16-bit expansion and richer console audio
the CD / early 3D era
modern large-scale orchestral and ambient soundtracks

This approach allows the dataset to function as a small but focused sample of soundtrack history rather than a complete archive.

title: Data
text:
Dataset Structure
Each row in the dataset represents one soundtrack track. The dataset includes the following variables:

game – the video game title
year – the release year of the game
era – the technological era the game belongs to
track title – the title of the soundtrack piece
Duration_sec – the track length converted into seconds

Track durations were standardized into seconds so they could be analyzed numerically across games and eras.

title: Data
text:
Why these Variables Matter
The main goal of the project is to examine how the scale and structure of video game soundtracks changed over time. Because of this, the most important variables are:

number of tracks per game
total soundtrack runtime
average track length
distribution of track lengths

These measures help show how soundtrack design expanded alongside improvements in hardware, storage, and audio production.

title: Data
text:
Analytical Approach
After constructing and cleaning the dataset, I grouped tracks by game in order to calculate summary measures such as:

total number of tracks per soundtrack
total runtime of each soundtrack
average track length

These metrics make it possible to compare soundtrack structure across technological eras and to test the broader idea that early games relied on shorter, more limited musical material while later games supported larger and more varied soundtrack design.

title: Analysis
text:
To compare soundtrack structure across eras, I use:

total number of tracks per game
total runtime per game
average track length
track-length distributions

These measures help show how soundtrack structure expanded as gaming technology evolved

library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

tracks <- read.csv("video_game_ost_cleaned_with_seconds.csv", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

game_summary <- tracks |>
  group_by(game, year, era) |>
  summarise(
    total_tracks = n(),
    total_runtime_sec = sum(Duration_sec, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_track_length = mean(Duration_sec, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(total_runtime_min = total_runtime_sec / 60)

game_summary

# A tibble: 13 × 7
   game               year era   total_tracks total_runtime_sec avg_track_length
   <chr>             <int> <chr>        <int>             <int>            <dbl>
 1 Halo               2001 CD_E…           11              1938            176. 
 2 Halo               2002 CD_E…            9               740             82.2
 3 Mega Man           1987 Chip…           16              2121            133. 
 4 Mega Man           1991 Chip…            2               263            132. 
 5 Metal Gear Solid   1998 CD_E…           19              3246            171. 
 6 Minecraft          2011 Mode…           24              3530            147. 
 7 Skyrim             2011 Mode…           15              3510            234  
 8 Sonic The Hedgeh…  1991 Chip…            7               776            111. 
 9 Super Mario 64     1997 CD_E…           20              2025            101. 
10 Super Mario Bros   1986 Chip…           16              1845            115. 
11 The Last of Us     2013 Mode…           15              1985            132. 
12 The Legend of Ze…  1986 Chip…           12              1118             93.2
13 The Legend of Ze…  1998 CD_E…           20              2688            134. 
# ℹ 1 more variable: total_runtime_min <dbl>


This table summarizes key structural characteristics of each game’s soundtrack. For every game in the dataset, I calculated the total number of tracks, the total runtime of the soundtrack in seconds, and the average length of individual tracks. Converting total runtime into minutes allows easier comparison across titles and eras. These measures provide a baseline view of how soundtrack scale varies across games released at different technological stages of video game history.



ggplot(game_summary, aes(x = year, y = total_tracks)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Number of Tracks per Game Over Time",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Number of Tracks"
  )








This plot shows how the number of tracks included in a game’s soundtrack changes over time. Each point represents a single game in the dataset, and the fitted trend line illustrates the overall direction of change. Early arcade and 8-bit titles typically contained relatively few tracks because hardware limitations restricted storage and sound channels. As gaming technology advanced, especially with CD-based systems and modern digital storage, soundtracks expanded dramatically. Modern games often include dozens of tracks, reflecting the shift toward more cinematic and atmospheric musical design.




ggplot(game_summary, aes(x = era, y = total_runtime_min)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Soundtrack Runtime by Era",
    x = "Era",
    y = "Runtime (minutes)"
  )








This boxplot compares the total runtime of game soundtracks across technological eras. The distribution highlights how early games tended to have shorter overall soundtracks, often consisting of brief looping musical cues. Later eras show significantly greater runtime variability and longer overall soundtracks. This change reflects the growing importance of music in shaping narrative atmosphere, exploration, and emotional tone in modern video games.




ggplot(tracks, aes(x = Duration_sec)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Track Lengths",
    x = "Track Duration (seconds)",
    y = "Count"
  )








The histogram shows the distribution of individual track lengths across the dataset. Track durations vary widely, ranging from very short pieces to extremely long compositions. Many tracks cluster around moderate durations, while a smaller number of outliers represent much longer pieces. This variation suggests that video game soundtracks can include a mix of short looping cues as well as longer compositions, reflecting the diverse musical structures used in game scoring.




avg_length_by_year <- game_summary |>
  group_by(year) |>
  summarise(avg_length = mean(avg_track_length, na.rm = TRUE))

ggplot(avg_length_by_year, aes(x = year, y = avg_length)) +
  geom_line(color = "#6aff6a", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = "#6aff6a", size = 3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "white") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Average Track Length Over Time",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Average Track Length (seconds)"
  )

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'









This visualization tracks how the average duration of individual soundtrack pieces changes over time. Early video game music often relied on short looping segments designed to repeat during gameplay, while later games increasingly featured longer compositions. The upward trend suggests a shift away from purely functional loops and toward more expansive soundtrack writing.

title: Analysis
text:
avg_length_by_year <- game_summary |>
  group_by(year) |>
  summarise(avg_length = mean(avg_track_length, na.rm = TRUE))

ggplot(avg_length_by_year, aes(x = year, y = avg_length)) +
  geom_line(color = "#6aff6a", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = "#6aff6a", size = 3) +

  labs(
    title = "Average Track Length Over Time",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Average Track Length (seconds)"
  )

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'









This visualization tracks how the average duration of individual soundtrack pieces changes over time. Early video game music often relied on short looping segments designed to repeat during gameplay, while later games increasingly featured longer compositions. The upward trend suggests a shift away from purely functional loops and toward more expansive soundtrack writing.

title: Conclusion
text: This project shows that video game soundtracks expanded dramatically over time, reflecting advances in storage capacity, audio hardware, and the growing cinematic ambitions of game design.

title: Conclusion
text: This project shows that video game soundtracks expanded dramatically over time, reflecting advances in storage capacity, audio hardware, and the growing cinematic ambitions of game design.

title: Conclusion
text:
Main Findings
The analysis highlights several trends across technological eras:

newer games tend to include more tracks per soundtrack
total soundtrack run time increased significantly over time
early games relied more heavily on short looping musical structures due to hardware limitations

Together, these patterns illustrate how technological constraints shaped the musical structure of early games, while modern systems allow far larger and more varied soundtracks.

title: Conclusion
text:
Interpretation
These results suggest that the evolution of video game music is closely tied to technological development. Early systems such as arcade machines and 8-bit consoles could only generate a small number of sound channels and had extremely limited storage capacity. As a result, composers created short, looping melodies designed to maximize musical impact within severe technical constraints.
As hardware improved through the 16-bit era, CD-based systems, and modern consoles, composers gained access to greater storage space, higher audio fidelity, and the ability to incorporate orchestral recordings and longer compositions. Modern games now frequently feature cinematic soundtracks that rival film scores in scale and complexity.

title: Conclusion
text:
Future Work
Future research could expand this analysis in several directions:

incorporating a larger data set of games across more genres and eras
extracting audio features such as tempo, instrumentation, or harmonic complexity
comparing soundtrack structure across different game franchises or developers

Such work would help further illuminate how technological change, artistic practice, and player expectations interact in shaping video game music.

title: Conclusion
text:
References
Collins, Karen. 2008. Game Sound: An Introduction to the History, Theory, and Practice of Video Game Music and Sound Design. MIT Press.
Collins, Karen. 2013. Playing with Sound: A Theory of Interacting with Sound and Music in Video Games. MIT Press.
Lerner, Neil, ed. 2013. Music in Video Games: Studying Play. Routledge.
Summers, Tim. 2016. Understanding Video Game Music. Cambridge University Press.
